title: RFK Racing says all four Fords had race-winning speed at Daytona 500
caption: RFK Racing speed
body:
RFK Racing reported that all four of its Fords demonstrated race-winning speed during a wild Daytona 500 finish. Brad Keselowski finished P5 after getting caught in a last-lap incident, while Lukus Wright finished P7 in an impressive debut. Ryan Preece showed top-10 pace until late damage, and Chris Buescher was fighting in the lead pack before the final-lap wreck. Despite the chaos, three RFK Fords finished inside the top 10, giving the team plenty of pride heading into the rest of the Cup Series calendar.

Keselowski's #6 entry spent the majority of the race lingering near the front, utilizing strategic drafting partnerships and excellently timed pit cycles to position himself for a late-race assault. However, as is almost destined at the Great American Race, a multi-car pileup in the final corners reshuffled the running order dramatically. The veteran driver and co-owner miraculously kept his Ford Mustang Dark Horse largely out of the wall, dragging it across the finish line to secure a top-five position that salvages massive points.

Equally impressive was the performance of newcomer Lukus Wright in the #99 car. Entering his first Daytona 500 under intense media scrutiny, Wright drove with the undeniable poise of a veteran, maintaining his spot in the highly turbulent lead draft and successfully weathering the inevitable "Big One." RFK Racing leaves Florida with validated confidence in their superspeedway program and their newly expanded four-car operation as the NASCAR Cup Series season kicks fully into gear.

The entire RFK garage was extremely vocal post-race about how well the Ford aerodynamic package behaved in the turbulent air of the draft. "We knew we had a rocket ship when we rolled off the truck on Thursday," Keselowski mentioned in his media availability. "The way these cars sucked up to the bumper of the leader gave us all the options in the world. It's incredibly validating for the whole shop back in Concord." The speed of the newly expanded four-car lineup proves that their massive offseason investments in simulation correlation are already paying immense dividends.

For Lukus Wright specifically, surviving Daytona was about more than just a good finish—it was about establishing respect within the tightly-knit drafting alliances. Multiple veterans commended Wright's predictable and smooth maneuvers at 190 mph, a vital trait that will serve him well at Talladega and Atlanta. Moving forward, the industry consensus is entirely clear: RFK Racing is no longer just a dark horse organization, but a deeply formidable, multi-car powerhouse capable of challenging Hendrick and Gibbs on any given Sunday.
